A1.text: People think that rich men who are not married want to find a wife. When a rich man comes to a new place, families want him to marry their daughters. The mothers in these families have one main goal. They want to find good husbands for their daughters. When they hear about a new rich man, they get very excited. They start planning how to meet him. The mothers talk to each other about this man. They wonder if he is nice and if he wants to get married. All the families hope their daughter will be the lucky one.

sentences.text: People think that rich men who are not married want to find a wife.
When a rich man comes to a new place, families want him to marry their daughters.
The mothers in these families have one main goal.
They want to find good husbands for their daughters.
When they hear about a new rich man, they get very excited.
They start planning how to meet him.
The mothers talk to each other about this man.
They wonder if he is nice and if he wants to get married.
All the families hope their daughter will be the lucky one.

B1.text: Everyone knows that a rich single man must want to find a wife. When such a man moves to a new place, the families around him believe that he should marry one of their daughters, even if they don't know anything about his feelings or opinions. This idea becomes the main concern of the neighboring mothers. They see this wealthy newcomer as the perfect solution to their daughters' futures. The news of a rich, unmarried gentleman creates immediate excitement in the community. Every mother begins to calculate her chances and plan her approach. Conversations between neighbors focus entirely on this mysterious new resident. They speculate about his character, his intentions, and his preferences in women. The competition among families becomes both subtle and intense.

sentences.text: Everyone knows that a rich single man must want to find a wife.
When such a man moves to a new place, the families around him believe that he should marry one of their daughters, even if they don't know anything about his feelings or opinions.
This idea becomes the main concern of the neighboring mothers.
They see this wealthy newcomer as the perfect solution to their daughters' futures.
The news of a rich, unmarried gentleman creates immediate excitement in the community.
Every mother begins to calculate her chances and plan her approach.
Conversations between neighbors focus entirely on this mysterious new resident.
They speculate about his character, his intentions, and his preferences in women.
The competition among families becomes both subtle and intense.

original.text: It is a truth universally acknowledged, that a single man in possession of a good fortune, must be in want of a wife. However little known the feelings or views of such a man may be on his first entering a neighbourhood, this truth is so well fixed in the minds of the surrounding families, that he is considered the rightful property of some one or other of their daughters. The advantage of this persuasion is, that it fixes the attention of the young man on the most eligible matches. My dear Mr. Bennet, said his lady to him one day, have you heard that Netherfield Park is let at last? Mr. Bennet replied that he had not. But it is, returned she; for Mrs. Long has just been here, and she told me all about it. Mr. Bennet made no answer. Do you not want to know who has taken it? cried his wife impatiently. You want to tell me, and I have no objection to hearing it.

However little known the feelings or views of such a man may be on his first entering a neighbourhood, this truth is so well fixed in the minds of the surrounding families, that he is considered the rightful property of some one or other of their daughters.
The advantage of this persuasion is, that it fixes the attention of the young man on the most eligible matches.
My dear Mr. Bennet, said his lady to him one day, have you heard that Netherfield Park is let at last?
Mr. Bennet replied that he had not.
But it is, returned she; for Mrs. Long has just been here, and she told me all about it.
Mr. Bennet made no answer.
Do you not want to know who has taken it? cried his wife impatiently.
